title: Stop Calling It Vibe Coding
author: Tim O'Brien
text:
Stop Calling It Vibe Coding
You’re telling on yourself
Four years ago, I was fighting a React Native project that broke every week. A new dependency complication. XCode is refusing to cooperate with Expo. Debugging across disconnected platforms that made you want to quit.
Now I skip React Native entirely and write Swift. I have a tool that catches the weird typos that used to cost me days of painful debugging. The edge cases that consumed years of a developer’s career are just… handled. Generative AI is helping, but I’m still writing and am responsible for the code. What it has removed is the “drudgery” of frameworks and configuration.
Software Engineering is Back
Alain Dichiappari published a piece called “Software Engineering is Back,” and it’s one of those rare posts that makes you want to stand up and slow clap. Not because it says something nobody’s thinking, but because it says what a lot of us have been living — and says it without the breathless hype that’s poisoned so much of the AI-in-software conversation.
His central thesis lands hard: the frameworks, the libraries, the layers of abstraction we’ve been stacking for years were never simplification. They were, as he puts it, “intellectual surrender.” He continues:
Antirez likes to call it “automated programming”, and I really like that framing. It captures the essence far better than the shallow, almost dismissive label of “vibe coding”. Automation was at the core of most of the work and cultural revolutions of human history. The printing press, the loom, the assembly line. This one doesn’t differ much.
Now that coding agents can handle the boilerplate, the grunt work, the mortar-spreading — we get to go back to being architects. Engineers. Thinkers.
“Most of my work is still there. I still have to deeply think about every important aspect of what I want to build. The architecture, the trade offs, the product decisions, the edge cases that will bite you at 3am. What’s gone is the tearing, exhausting manual labour of typing every single line of code.” — Alain Dichiappari
Read that again. This is not hands-off. This is not “vibe coding.” This is someone with twenty years of experience doing more engineering, not less — just with dramatically better leverage.
This resonates with me because it’s what I’ve been trying to articulate for months. This is work. It’s not easier — but the drudgework is gone, and what’s left is the part that actually matters.
Automated Programming, Not Autonomous Programming
Alain credits Antirez for the framing of “automated programming,” and I think that’s exactly right. I’ve been using the term “supervised generation” to describe how I actually work with these tools — and the distinction matters enormously.
Supervised generation means the human is present, engaged, and making decisions. Not at every keystroke anymore, but at every architectural junction, every design tradeoff, every moment where judgment is required. The AI generates. You supervise. You accept, reject, or redirect. This is what Alain describes when he talks about being the architect who no longer has to lay every brick — but who absolutely knows when a brick is wrong.
This week’s This Day in AI podcast (EP99.33) made the same point from a different angle. The hosts described the next evolution of productive AI use as establishing quality criteria for subtasks, having the AI filter its own work against those criteria, and only presenting you with candidates that meet the bar. That’s not vibing. That’s supervision. That’s engineering management applied to AI agents.
The people calling this “vibe coding” are telling on themselves. If your process is vibes, you’re not using the tools well. You’re not setting up your environment meticulously. You’re not establishing criteria. You’re not reviewing with intent. You’re just hoping the slot machine pays out, and then posting about it on X when it does.
Frameworks Were Never About Engineering
Alain’s most cutting observation is his third reason for frameworks: labor costs. Companies adopted React, Next.js, and the Vercel ecosystem not because it was better engineering, but because it let them hire “React Developers” instead of software engineers. Plug and play. Easy to replace. A cog in someone else’s machine.
This resonates with something I’ve been writing about for years — what I call Tool Syndrome. The industry’s chronic obsession with tooling over thinking. We fetishize the framework, the library, the managed service, because it lets us avoid the harder work of understanding the problem. And now, ironically, AI is making it possible to shed all that accumulated complexity and get back to first principles.
As Alain writes: “You solve the problem you have, not the problem someone on a conference stage told you that you’ll eventually have.”
Having used many frameworks, I’ve found they tend to become overly complex, to the point where you need a framework to manage the framework. With the tools that are now available, it’s more than possible to skip the frameworks and focus on the end product.
Where This Goes Next
The This Day in AI hosts asked a provocative question: “Does the future really come down to how efficiently you can turn tokens into money?” I think the answer is no — or at least, not in the way the hype cycle suggests. The future is about how effectively you can apply human judgment at the right moments in an increasingly automated pipeline. The tokens are cheap. The judgment is not.
The engineers who thrive in this world won’t be the ones who learned to “vibe” with AI. They’ll be the ones who, like Alain, have twenty years of bricks-and-mortar experience and finally got handed a tool that lets them focus on what they were always best at: thinking clearly about hard problems.
Software engineering is back. It never really left. We just buried it under a decade of frameworks and forgot what it looked like.
This post references Software Engineering is Back by Alain Dichiappari (Feb 7, 2026) and This Day in AI Podcast EP99.33: “Is the ChatGPT Era Over? Opus 4.6 & The Shift from Chat to Delegation” (Feb 5, 2026).
